title: Quiz: Dynamic Array Reconfiguration
description: Test your understanding of atom sorting, transport dynamics, and reconfigurable connectivity in neutral atom systems

questions.question: Why is defect-free array assembly necessary for neutral atom quantum computing?

options.text: Because atoms repel each other and cannot be placed close together
Because stochastic loading gives only ~50% probability per site, making perfect loading exponentially unlikely
Because the laser system can only address alternating sites
Because quantum error correction requires specific atom arrangements

questions.explanation: Each optical tweezer site has approximately 50% probability of containing exactly one atom due to light-assisted collisions. For N sites, the probability of all sites being filled is (0.5)^N, which becomes negligibly small for practical qubit numbers (e.g., 10^-30 for 100 qubits). Defect-free assembly solves this by loading more traps and sorting atoms.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of using minimum-jerk or minimum-snap trajectories for atom transport?

options.text: They require less laser power
They minimize heating by avoiding sudden accelerations
They are faster than constant velocity transport
They allow transport without any trap

questions.explanation: Minimum-jerk and minimum-snap trajectories provide smooth acceleration profiles that avoid sudden changes. This keeps the transport adiabatic (slow compared to trap oscillation), minimizing excitation of motional states and thus reducing heating. Sudden acceleration at endpoints would cause significant heating.

questions.question: In the context of atom transport, what does the adiabaticity parameter eta measure?

options.text: The ratio of trap depth to atomic temperature
The ratio of transport time to trap oscillation period
The ratio of atom mass to photon momentum
The ratio of laser power to scattering rate

questions.explanation: The adiabaticity parameter is eta = omega_trap * T_transport, comparing the transport timescale to the characteristic trap oscillation period. When eta >> 1, the transport is adiabatic - the atom remains in the trap ground state throughout. When eta ~ 1, significant motional excitation occurs.

questions.question: How do neutral atoms achieve arbitrary qubit connectivity compared to fixed architectures like superconducting qubits?

options.text: By using longer-range interactions that reach all qubits
By physically moving atoms to bring any pair within interaction range
By using photon-mediated interactions through a shared cavity
By performing virtual SWAP gates through classical computation

questions.explanation: Neutral atoms achieve arbitrary connectivity by physically rearranging the atom array. Using AOD-controlled optical tweezers, atoms can be transported to bring any desired pair within the Rydberg blockade radius for two-qubit gates. This eliminates the need for SWAP gate overheads present in fixed-connectivity architectures.

questions.question: What is the typical loading probability for a single atom in an optical tweezer from a MOT?

options.text: Nearly 100% with careful alignment
Approximately 50% due to light-assisted collision parity projection
Approximately 25% due to limited MOT density
Variable from 0-100% depending on trap depth

questions.explanation: Light-assisted collisions cause 'parity projection' - when two atoms occupy the same trap, near-resonant light causes them to collide and both are ejected. This ensures at most one atom per trap but results in approximately 50% loading probability (either 0 or 1 atom with roughly equal probability).

questions.question: Which component typically dominates the total time for defect-free array assembly?

options.text: Atom transport between sites
Fluorescence imaging to detect occupied sites
Classical computation for optimal sorting
AOD frequency switching

questions.explanation: Fluorescence imaging typically requires 10-50 ms to collect enough photons for reliable atom detection, while transport takes ~100 us per move and classical computation takes a few ms. For a 100-atom array, total transport time is ~5 ms, making imaging the dominant time component at ~30 ms.

questions.question: Parallel row-column sorting algorithms can significantly reduce the total number of sequential move operations required to assemble a 2D array.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. Row-column sorting exploits the fact that atoms in different rows (or columns) can move simultaneously without interfering. By first sorting all rows in parallel, then all columns in parallel, the number of sequential operations is reduced from O(N) to O(sqrt(N)) for an N-atom square array.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid mechanisms for atom loss during transport? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Background gas collisions knocking atoms from traps
Non-adiabatic transport causing heating above trap depth
Trap depth variations along the transport path
Spontaneous decay from the atomic ground state

questions.explanation: Atom loss during transport can result from (a) background gas collisions, (b) heating from non-adiabatic transport that gives atoms enough energy to escape, and (c) variations in trap depth that may be insufficient at certain points. Ground state atoms (d) do not spontaneously decay - they are stable indefinitely.
